one_line_takeaway: TensorFlow's own persistent-but-undocumented-for-I/O APIs (debug ops, dataset readers, distributed RPC calls) let attackers hide fully functional exfiltration and malware-dropper payloads inside SavedModel files that evade current Hugging Face/TensorFlow Hub scanners, motivating (but not yet validating) an LLM-based semantic graph scanner as a second-layer defense.
key_finding: TensorFlow ships with persistent (serializable) APIs -- including debug/logging ops like PrintV2 and DebugIdentity, dataset ops like FixedLengthRecordDatasetV2, and the distributed RPC client/server ops (rpc_client/rpc_call) -- whose documented purpose is unrelated to file or network I/O but which can be composed inside a SavedModel's computational graph to implement all four 'attack core functions' (file read, file write, network send, network receive), and this abuse is not caught by current model-hub scanners at Hugging Face and TensorFlow Hub because those scanners perform syntactic matching against a known list of dangerous function names rather than semantic analysis of a model's op graph. The authors demonstrate this concretely with two working PoC attacks (secret-file exfiltration via RPC, and a malware dropper that persists via .bashrc) hidden inside an otherwise-innocuous linear-regression model.
core_contribution: The paper (Nabeel & Starov, Palo Alto Networks, Virus Bulletin Conference, Sept 2025) demonstrates that TensorFlow's own APIs contain 'hidden' or latent core capabilities (file read, file write, network send, network receive) that are not designed for I/O but can be repurposed by an attacker to build stealthy malware inside a serialized SavedModel, bypassing existing model-hub scanners (Hugging Face, TensorFlow Hub) that rely on syntactic/signature detection of explicitly dangerous APIs (e.g. ReadFile, WriteFile, gRPC calls). To find these hidden APIs at scale, the authors build a ReAct-style LLM agent (supervisor LLM + 4 tools: a serializable-function extractor, a hidden-core-function detector, a TensorFlow API-doc RAG tool, and a general web-search tool) that analyzes the full TensorFlow 2.18.0 source tree to identify which APIs are (a) persistent/serializable into a SavedModel and (b) abusable for one of the four attack core functions. The agent's output is a 'Serializable Hidden Core Functions Report' (human-verified) listing concrete abusable APIs, e.g. tf.raw_ops.FixedLengthRecordDatasetV2 and tf.raw_ops.InitializeTableFromTextFile for file read, tf.raw_ops.SaveSlices and tf.raw_ops.PrintV2 for file write, and TensorFlow's distributed RPC ops (gen_rpc_ops.rpc_client/rpc_call) plus tf.raw_ops.DebugIdentity for network receive/send. Using these primitives the authors build and demonstrate two working proof-of-concept attacks embedded in a toy tf.Module linear-regression model saved via tf.saved_model.save: (1) a data-exfiltration attack that reads a secret file via FixedLengthRecordDatasetV2 and sends its contents over gen_rpc_ops.rpc_call to an attacker server, and (2) a malware-dropper attack that fetches a payload via rpc_call, writes it to disk with tf.raw_ops.PrintV2 (as e.g. ~/evil.py), and appends an execution command to the victim's .bashrc for persistence/execution on next shell refresh. Finally, as a countermeasure, the authors propose (but do not build/evaluate empirically in this text) an LLM-based second-layer scanner: after a model passes conventional signature-based scanning, its SavedModel computational graph and metadata are extracted and fed to an LLM prompted with a 3-step chain-of-thought (identify candidate core-attack-function ops in the graph -> analyse the sequence/chaining of ops for logical attack patterns, e.g. MatchingFiles -> FixedLengthRecordDataset -> DebugIdentity as an exfiltration chain -> issue a malicious/benign verdict with an explanation of the discovered attack chain).
threat_model: Software/AI supply-chain attacker who uploads a poisoned pre-trained model (TensorFlow SavedModel format) to a public model hub such as Hugging Face or TensorFlow Hub. The malicious functionality is embedded not via known-dangerous APIs or known deserialization bugs (pickle RCE, Keras Lambda-layer injection) but via legitimate, persistent TensorFlow ops whose I/O/network side effects are non-obvious/undocumented as such (e.g. debug/logging ops, distributed-RPC ops, dataset-reading ops). The victim is a developer/organization that downloads the model and loads/runs it (tf.saved_model.load + inference call), at which point the embedded code executes with the victim's privileges, enabling file exfiltration, malware dropping, and (per the paper's stated but not demonstrated extension) RCE/ransomware. The paper explicitly targets the blind spot of current model-hub scanners, which perform syntactic/signature matching on known dangerous function names and therefore miss semantically-hidden abuse of otherwise 'benign' APIs.
limitations: The demonstrated attacks use a toy linear-regression model and Linux-specific persistence (.bashrc) rather than production-scale models or cross-platform payloads. The paper explicitly states it lacks a 'direct execution primitive' -- the dropper attack requires the victim's shell to be refreshed to trigger execution, rather than achieving immediate code execution. The proposed LLM-based defensive scanner is described only at a design/prompt-engineering level (3-step chain-of-thought over the extracted graph); the paper reports no implementation results, no detection/false-positive rate, no dataset of benign vs malicious models used for evaluation, and no comparison against existing scanners' actual detection numbers -- it is a proposed mitigation, not a validated one. The hidden-core-function discovery process itself is LLM-generated and human-verified rather than exhaustively/formally proven complete; the authors note the LLM sometimes produces non-functional PoC code for rarely used APIs, requiring human-in-the-loop correction, implying the automated discovery is not fully reliable on its own. The work covers only TensorFlow (version 2.18.0); PyTorch and other frameworks are mentioned as future extensions but not analyzed.
